content.problemStatement:
A thermal decomposition reaction is breakdown of a compound into one or more compounds when heated.

For example, P→a⋅Q+b⋅RP \rightarrow a\cdot Q + b\cdot RP→a⋅Q+b⋅R is a decomposition reaction. Here, 111 unit of PPP is converted into aaa unit of QQQ and bbb unit of RRR after completion of the reaction.

You are given NNN compounds numbered from 111 to NNN. The initial amount of the ithi^{th}ith compound is QiQ_iQi​.

You are also given MMM equations. Every equation is of the form:
C0→W1⋅C1+W2⋅C2+…+WX⋅CXC_0 \rightarrow W_1\cdot C_1 + W_2\cdot C_2 + \ldots + W_X\cdot C_XC0​→W1​⋅C1​+W2​⋅C2​+…+WX​⋅CX​.

Here:


C0C_0C0​ represents the compound which will decompose into XXX compounds on heating.
CiC_iCi​ (1≤i≤X)(1 \leq i \leq X )(1≤i≤X) represents the compounds into which C0C_0C0​ is decomposed.
WiW_iWi​ (1≤i≤X)(1 \leq i \leq X )(1≤i≤X) represents the number of units of CiC_iCi​ produced due to the decomposition of C0C_0C0​.
Ci>Ci−1C_i > C_{i-1}Ci​>Ci−1​ for all (1≤i≤X)(1 \leq i \leq X)(1≤i≤X).
Note that a compound can decompose in **at most one** way only.

Find the final amount of each compound after **prolonged heating**. Since the number can be huge, print the answer modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

First line will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM - the number of compounds and the number of equations.
Second line will contain NNN space separated integers Q1,Q2,...,QNQ_1, Q_2, ..., Q_NQ1​,Q2​,...,QN​ - the initial amounts of each compound.
The next 2⋅M2\cdot M2⋅M lines contain the details of the MMM equations.  Each equation is represented using 222 lines.
First line of the ithi^{th}ith equation contains two space separated integers Ci,0C_{i,0}Ci,0​, XiX_iXi​ - the decomposing compound and the number of compounds in which it decomposes.
Second line of the ithi^{th}ith equation contains 2⋅Xi2 \cdot X_i2⋅Xi​ space separated integers Wi,1,Ci,1,Wi,2,Ci,2,…,Wi,Xi,Ci,XiW_{i,1}, C_{i,1}, W_{i,2}, C_{i,2},… ,W_{i,X_i}, C_{i,X_i}Wi,1​,Ci,1​,Wi,2​,Ci,2​,…,Wi,Xi​​,Ci,Xi​​ - the number of units and the types of compounds into which Ci,0C_{i,0}Ci,0​ decomposes.


Output Format
Print NNN lines. The ithi^{th}ith line contains a single integer denoting the final amount of the ithi^{th}ith compound after prolonged heating modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: Sum of all X over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two space‑separated integers N and M — the number of compounds and the number of decomposition equations.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers Q1,Q2,…,QN — the initial amount of each compound.
- Then M blocks follow, each describing one equation.
  * The first line of a block contains two integers C0 and X — the compound that decomposes and the number of products.
  * The second line contains 2·X integers: W1 C1 W2 C2 … WX CX, where Wi is the number of units of compound Ci produced from one unit of C0. The product compounds are given in strictly increasing order.
All indices are 1‑based. A compound appears in at most one equation.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines. The i‑th line contains a single integer – the final amount of compound i after all possible decompositions, modulo 1 000 000 007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Compound 1 decomposes into 3·2 + 4·3, producing 15 of compound 2 and 20 of compound 3. After that compound 3 (now 32 units) decomposes into 2·4, adding 64 to compound 4. Final amounts: [0,25,0,66].
Only compound 3 decomposes: 1 unit → 5 units of compound 4 and 7 units of compound 5. No further reactions.
No decomposition equations, so amounts stay unchanged.

solution.approach:
Because every equation decomposes a compound into only compounds with larger indices (Ci > C0) and each compound can decompose at most once, the dependency graph is a DAG where edges go from smaller to larger indices. We can therefore process compounds in decreasing order of their index. For a compound i we already know its final amount (including amounts produced by higher‑indexed compounds). If i has an equation, all its units will instantly decompose, adding Q[i]·Wi to each product compound Ci. After distributing the amount we set Q[i] to zero. Repeating this from N down to 1 yields the final amounts for all compounds.
The algorithm:
1. Read the data and store for each compound i (if any) a list of pairs (weight, product).
2. Iterate i from N down to 1:
   - If i has an equation, for each (w, c) do Q[c] = (Q[c] + Q[i] * w) mod MOD.
   - Set Q[i] = 0.
3. Output the resulting Q array.
The modulo operation is applied after each addition to avoid overflow. The total work is O(N + ΣXi) which fits the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + total number of products) per test case, i.e., O(N + ΣXi).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + ΣXi) to store the amounts and adjacency lists.
